content.problemStatement:
Words that contain many consecutive consonants, like "`schtschurowskia`", are generally considered somewhat hard to pronounce.

We say that a word is hard to pronounce if it contains 444 or more consonants in a row; otherwise it is easy to pronounce. For example, "apple" and "polish" are easy to pronounce, but "schtschurowskia" is hard to pronounce.

You are given a string SSS consisting of NNN lowercase Latin characters. Determine whether it is easy to pronounce or not based on the rule above — print `YES` if it is easy to pronounce and `NO` otherwise.

For the purposes of this problem, the vowels are the characters {a,e,i,o,u}\{a, e, i, o, u\}{a,e,i,o,u} and the consonants are the other 212121 characters.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of string SSS.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer — `YES` if SSS is easy to pronounce, and `NO` otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase. For example, the strings `YES`, `yeS`, `yes`, and `YeS` will all be treated as identical.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the string; the second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase Latin letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the word is easy to pronounce (no run of 4 or more consecutive consonants) or "NO" otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: apple has no run of 4 consonants → YES; schtschurowskia contains a run of 6 consonants → NO; polish is easy → YES; tryst has 5 consecutive consonants → NO; cry has only 3 consonants → YES.
bcdf is 4 consecutive consonants → hard → NO; abca has at most 1 consecutive consonant between vowels → easy → YES.
The substring "dfgh" is 4 consecutive consonants, so the word is hard.

solution.approach: For each string we scan it from left to right, keeping a counter of the current length of a consecutive consonant block. When a vowel (a, e, i, o, u) is seen the counter is reset to zero. If the counter ever reaches 4, the word is hard to pronounce and we can stop scanning. After the scan we output YES if we never reached 4, otherwise NO. This runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) where N is the length of each string (max 100).

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int N; char s[105]; scanf("%d",&N); scanf("%s",s); int cnt=0, hard=0; for(int i=0;s[i];++i){char c=s[i]; if(c=='a'||c=='e'||c=='i'||c=='o'||c=='u') cnt=0; else{cnt++; if(cnt>=4){hard=1; break;}} } printf("%s\n", hard?"NO":"YES"); } return 0; }
